title: TECOS
subtitle: Sitagliptin Cardiovascular Outcomes — Summary, Why/How, MCQ & T/F Module
authors: Green JB, Bethel MA, Armstrong PW, et al.; N Engl J Med 2015

trialDesign.fullName: Trial Evaluating Cardiovascular Outcomes with Sitagliptin (TECOS)
trialDesign.design: Randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled, event-driven cardiovascular outcomes trial
trialDesign.population: 14,671 patients with type 2 diabetes, HbA1c 6.5–8.0%, and established cardiovascular disease
trialDesign.intervention: Sitagliptin 100 mg daily (50 mg if eGFR 30–<50) added to usual care
trialDesign.comparator: Placebo added to usual care with open-label antihyperglycemic agents as needed
trialDesign.primaryEndpoint: First confirmed CV death, nonfatal MI, nonfatal stroke, or hospitalization for unstable angina
trialDesign.overview: Sitagliptin was noninferior for major cardiovascular events (HR 0.98) with no excess heart failure hospitalization (HR 1.00).

headings.background: Regulatory requirement to prove cardiovascular safety of new antihyperglycemic agents; sitagliptin DPP-4 inhibitor data lacking.
headings.design: 673 sites, 38 countries; double-blind; event-driven; noninferiority margin 1.30.
headings.population: Mean diabetes duration 11.6 years; established CVD required; eGFR ≥30.
headings.intervention: Sitagliptin 100 mg (or 50 mg by renal function) plus usual care.
headings.comparator: Placebo plus usual care; open-label glucose-lowering agents encouraged.
headings.followUp: Median 3.0 years; ~26–27% discontinued study drug; 95.1% completed in sitagliptin arm.

outcomes.label: Primary composite MACE
outcomes.interpretation: 839 vs 851 events; no increased CV risk

safetyHighlights: No significant increase in MACE, HF, severe hypoglycemia, or all-cause death
Pancreatic cancer numerically lower with sitagliptin; pancreatitis not significantly increased
trialSummary.clinicalBottomLine: Sitagliptin added to usual care is cardiovascularly safe in high-risk type 2 diabetes, with neutral heart failure risk and modest glycemic benefit.

inclusionCriteria: Type 2 diabetes; HbA1c 6.5–8.0%; age ≥50 (or ≥21 with CVD risk factors)
Established cardiovascular disease; eGFR ≥30 ml/min/1.73 m²
exclusionCriteria: GLP-1 receptor agonist, DPP-4 inhibitor (other than sitagliptin), or thiazolidinedione (except pioglitazone) in prior 3 months
≥2 severe hypoglycemia episodes in prior 12 months; eGFR <30
keyTakeaways: Noninferior for MACE (HR 0.98); HF hospitalization neutral (HR 1.00).
HbA1c -0.29%; fewer add-on agents and less insulin with sitagliptin.
No significant pancreatitis, pancreatic cancer, or severe hypoglycemia signal.

items.title: TECOS — Main Points (High-Yield Overview)
items.content:
Background and Objective: Sitagliptin is an oral DPP-4 inhibitor that prolongs incretin action. TECOS evaluated long-term cardiovascular safety of adding sitagliptin to usual care in type 2 diabetes with established cardiovascular disease.

Methodology: 14,671 patients randomized to sitagliptin (100 mg or 50 mg daily by eGFR) or placebo. Primary composite: CV death, nonfatal MI, nonfatal stroke, or hospitalization for unstable angina.

Glycemic Control: Median follow-up 3.0 years; HbA1c difference -0.29 percentage points favoring sitagliptin. Fewer additional antihyperglycemic agents and less long-term insulin initiation with sitagliptin.

Cardiovascular Outcomes: Noninferior to placebo for primary composite (HR 0.98; P<0.001 for noninferiority). HF hospitalization identical (HR 1.00)—key safety differentiator vs saxagliptin.

Safety Profile: No increase in MACE, acute pancreatitis, pancreatic cancer, severe hypoglycemia, or all-cause death.
keyPoints: Primary composite HR 0.98 — noninferior; HF hospitalization HR 1.00
HbA1c -0.29%; fewer add-on agents and less insulin initiation
No signal for pancreatitis, pancreatic cancer, or severe hypoglycemia
items.reference: Green JB, Bethel MA, Armstrong PW, et al. Effect of sitagliptin on cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2015;373:232-242.

items.question: Why was the TECOS trial initiated?
items.answer: The trial was initiated to evaluate the long-term cardiovascular safety of sitagliptin, particularly in response to regulatory agencies requiring proof that new antihyperglycemic agents do not cause clinically meaningful increases in major adverse cardiovascular events.
keyPoints: The trial was initiated to evaluate the long-term cardiovascular safety of sitagliptin, particularly in response to regulatory agencies requiring proof that new antihyperglycemic agents do not cause clinically meaningful increases in major adverse cardiovascular events.
items.reference: TECOS Q1: The trial was initiated to evaluate the long-term cardiovascular safety of sitag... — Green JB, Bethel MA, Armstrong PW, et al. Effect of sitagliptin on cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2015;373:232-242.

items.question: How does sitagliptin physiologically improve glycemic control?
items.answer: Sitagliptin acts as a DPP-4 inhibitor that prolongs the action of incretin hormones, primarily suppressing glucagon levels and increasing endogenous insulin secretion.
keyPoints: Sitagliptin acts as a DPP-4 inhibitor that prolongs the action of incretin hormones, primarily suppressing glucagon levels and increasing endogenous insulin secretion.
items.reference: TECOS Q2: Sitagliptin acts as a DPP-4 inhibitor that prolongs the action of incretin hormo... — Green JB, Bethel MA, Armstrong PW, et al. Effect of sitagliptin on cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2015;373:232-242.

items.question: Why did the researchers encourage the use of open-label antihyperglycemic agents during the trial?
items.answer: This approach aimed to achieve individually appropriate glycated hemoglobin targets for all patients, thereby minimizing the potential confounding effects of differential glucose control between the two study groups.
keyPoints: This approach aimed to achieve individually appropriate glycated hemoglobin targets for all patients, thereby minimizing the potential confounding effects of differential glucose control between the two study groups.
items.reference: TECOS Q3: This approach aimed to achieve individually appropriate glycated hemoglobin targ... — Green JB, Bethel MA, Armstrong PW, et al. Effect of sitagliptin on cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2015;373:232-242.

items.question: How was the primary composite cardiovascular outcome formally defined?
items.answer: It was defined as the first confirmed event of cardiovascular death, nonfatal myocardial infarction, nonfatal stroke, or hospitalization for unstable angina.
keyPoints: It was defined as the first confirmed event of cardiovascular death, nonfatal myocardial infarction, nonfatal stroke, or hospitalization for unstable angina.
items.reference: TECOS Q4: It was defined as the first confirmed event of cardiovascular death, nonfatal my... — Green JB, Bethel MA, Armstrong PW, et al. Effect of sitagliptin on cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2015;373:232-242.

items.question: Why was the finding regarding hospitalization for heart failure highly significant in the context of this drug class?
items.answer: Previous cardiovascular outcome trials for other DPP-4 inhibitors, such as saxagliptin, showed an unexpected excess rate of hospitalization for heart failure; TECOS demonstrated no difference (hazard ratio 1.00), serving as a crucial safety differentiator.
keyPoints: Previous cardiovascular outcome trials for other DPP-4 inhibitors, such as saxagliptin, showed an unexpected excess rate of hospitalization for heart failure; TECOS demonstrated no difference (hazard ratio 1.00), serving as a crucial safety differentiator.
items.reference: TECOS Q5: Previous cardiovascular outcome trials for other DPP-4 inhibitors, such as saxag... — Green JB, Bethel MA, Armstrong PW, et al. Effect of sitagliptin on cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2015;373:232-242.

items.question: How did sitagliptin treatment affect patients' reliance on additional diabetes therapies?
items.answer: Patients in the sitagliptin group received significantly fewer additional antihyperglycemic agents and were less likely to initiate long-term insulin therapy compared to the placebo group over the study period.
keyPoints: Patients in the sitagliptin group received significantly fewer additional antihyperglycemic agents and were less likely to initiate long-term insulin therapy compared to the placebo group over the study period.
items.reference: TECOS Q6: Patients in the sitagliptin group received significantly fewer additional antihy... — Green JB, Bethel MA, Armstrong PW, et al. Effect of sitagliptin on cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2015;373:232-242.

items.question: Why did the trial utilize a noninferiority hypothesis as its primary statistical goal?
items.answer: The primary objective was establishing safety—to mathematically prove that adding sitagliptin did not increase the cardiovascular risk compared to a placebo, utilizing a relative risk upper boundary of 1.30.
keyPoints: The primary objective was establishing safety—to mathematically prove that adding sitagliptin did not increase the cardiovascular risk compared to a placebo, utilizing a relative risk upper boundary of 1.30.
items.reference: TECOS Q7: The primary objective was establishing safety—to mathematically prove that addin... — Green JB, Bethel MA, Armstrong PW, et al. Effect of sitagliptin on cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2015;373:232-242.

items.question: How was the data regarding specific events like acute pancreatitis and pancreatic cancer managed to ensure objectivity?
items.answer: An independent clinical events classification committee, whose members remained completely unaware of study-group assignments, adjudicated all events of acute pancreatitis and cancer.
keyPoints: An independent clinical events classification committee, whose members remained completely unaware of study-group assignments, adjudicated all events of acute pancreatitis and cancer.
items.reference: TECOS Q8: An independent clinical events classification committee, whose members remained ... — Green JB, Bethel MA, Armstrong PW, et al. Effect of sitagliptin on cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2015;373:232-242.

items.question: Why did the trial strictly exclude patients who had two or more episodes of severe hypoglycemia in the preceding 12 months?
items.answer: This exclusion criterion protected highly vulnerable patients, and the study required mandatory discontinuation of the study medication if a patient experienced severe hypoglycemia between visits despite adjustments to other therapies.
keyPoints: This exclusion criterion protected highly vulnerable patients, and the study required mandatory discontinuation of the study medication if a patient experienced severe hypoglycemia between visits despite adjustments to other therapies.
items.reference: TECOS Q9: This exclusion criterion protected highly vulnerable patients, and the study req... — Green JB, Bethel MA, Armstrong PW, et al. Effect of sitagliptin on cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2015;373:232-242.

items.question: How did the initial difference in glycated hemoglobin levels evolve over the 3-year median follow-up?
items.answer: At 4 months, HbA1c values were 0.4 percentage points lower in the sitagliptin group, but this difference narrowed over time to a final overall least-squares mean difference of -0.29 percentage points.
keyPoints: At 4 months, HbA1c values were 0.4 percentage points lower in the sitagliptin group, but this difference narrowed over time to a final overall least-squares mean difference of -0.29 percentage points.
items.reference: TECOS Q10: At 4 months, HbA1c values were 0.4 percentage points lower in the sitagliptin gr... — Green JB, Bethel MA, Armstrong PW, et al. Effect of sitagliptin on cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2015;373:232-242.

items.question: Why do the authors hypothesize that the heart failure results in TECOS differed from the SAVOR-TIMI 53 trial?
items.answer: They suggest the disparity could be due to differences in the enrolled patient populations, background clinical care, specific definitions of heart-failure events, intrinsic pharmacologic differences among the DPP-4 inhibitors, or simply the play of chance.
keyPoints: They suggest the disparity could be due to differences in the enrolled patient populations, background clinical care, specific definitions of heart-failure events, intrinsic pharmacologic differences among the DPP-4 inhibitors, or simply the play of chance.
items.reference: TECOS Q11: They suggest the disparity could be due to differences in the enrolled patient p... — Green JB, Bethel MA, Armstrong PW, et al. Effect of sitagliptin on cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2015;373:232-242.

items.question: How was the baseline estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR) factored into the dosing protocol for sitagliptin?
items.answer: Patients were normally assigned a dose of 100 mg daily; however, this was reduced to 50 mg daily if their baseline eGFR was between 30 and less than 50 ml per minute per 1.73 m².
keyPoints: Patients were normally assigned a dose of 100 mg daily; however, this was reduced to 50 mg daily if their baseline eGFR was between 30 and less than 50 ml per minute per 1.73 m².
items.reference: TECOS Q12: Patients were normally assigned a dose of 100 mg daily; however, this was reduce... — Green JB, Bethel MA, Armstrong PW, et al. Effect of sitagliptin on cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2015;373:232-242.

items.question: Why were events like expected diabetes-related complications not reported directly as standard adverse events?
items.answer: These expected complications were prespecified events of clinical interest that were already being collected systematically by the researchers, rendering redundant reporting unnecessary.
keyPoints: These expected complications were prespecified events of clinical interest that were already being collected systematically by the researchers, rendering redundant reporting unnecessary.
items.reference: TECOS Q13: These expected complications were prespecified events of clinical interest that ... — Green JB, Bethel MA, Armstrong PW, et al. Effect of sitagliptin on cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2015;373:232-242.

items.question: How did the intention-to-treat analysis for the primary outcome compare statistically to the per-protocol analysis?
items.answer: Both analytical models yielded identical hazard ratios of 0.98, strongly confirming the robustness of the finding that there was no significant between-group difference.
keyPoints: Both analytical models yielded identical hazard ratios of 0.98, strongly confirming the robustness of the finding that there was no significant between-group difference.
items.reference: TECOS Q14: Both analytical models yielded identical hazard ratios of 0.98, strongly confirm... — Green JB, Bethel MA, Armstrong PW, et al. Effect of sitagliptin on cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2015;373:232-242.

items.question: Why might the opportunistic approach to data collection be considered a limitation of this trial?
items.answer: The opportunistic approach meant that certain clinical values, such as the ratio of urinary albumin to creatinine, were not systematically acquired across the entire cohort, limiting the ability to analyze these specific renal parameters.
keyPoints: The opportunistic approach meant that certain clinical values, such as the ratio of urinary albumin to creatinine, were not systematically acquired across the entire cohort, limiting the ability to analyze these specific renal parameters.
items.reference: TECOS Q15: The opportunistic approach meant that certain clinical values, such as the ratio... — Green JB, Bethel MA, Armstrong PW, et al. Effect of sitagliptin on cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2015;373:232-242.

items.question: What was the median duration of follow-up for patients in the TECOS trial?
items.explanation: Median follow-up was 3.0 years.
items.reference: TECOS MCQ 1: Median follow-up was 3.0 years. — Green JB, Bethel MA, Armstrong PW, et al. Effect of sitagliptin on cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2015;373:232-242.

items.question: Which of the following drug classes was specifically excluded from use in the 3 months prior to patient enrollment?
items.explanation: GLP-1 receptor agonists were excluded in the prior 3 months.
items.reference: TECOS MCQ 2: GLP-1 receptor agonists were excluded in the prior 3 months. — Green JB, Bethel MA, Armstrong PW, et al. Effect of sitagliptin on cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2015;373:232-242.

items.question: What was the required baseline glycated hemoglobin (HbA1c) level for eligible patients entering the trial?
items.explanation: Baseline HbA1c requirement was 6.5–8.0%.
items.reference: TECOS MCQ 3: Baseline HbA1c requirement was 6.5–8.0%. — Green JB, Bethel MA, Armstrong PW, et al. Effect of sitagliptin on cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2015;373:232-242.

items.question: What was the hazard ratio for the primary composite cardiovascular outcome in the sitagliptin group versus placebo?
items.explanation: Primary composite HR was 0.98 (noninferior).
items.reference: TECOS MCQ 4: Primary composite HR was 0.98 (noninferior). — Green JB, Bethel MA, Armstrong PW, et al. Effect of sitagliptin on cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2015;373:232-242.

items.question: How did the rate of hospitalization for heart failure in the sitagliptin group mathematically compare to the placebo group?
options: It was significantly lower in the sitagliptin group (3.1% vs 5.2%).
It was practically identical (3.1% in both groups, HR 1.00).
It was slightly higher but not statistically significant.
It was significantly higher in the sitagliptin group.
items.explanation: HF hospitalization: 3.1% both groups; HR 1.00.
items.reference: TECOS MCQ 5: HF hospitalization: 3.1% both groups; HR 1.00. — Green JB, Bethel MA, Armstrong PW, et al. Effect of sitagliptin on cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2015;373:232-242.

items.question: What minimum estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR) was required for a patient to be included in the trial at baseline?
items.explanation: Minimum baseline eGFR was ≥30 ml/min/1.73 m².
items.reference: TECOS MCQ 6: Minimum baseline eGFR was ≥30 ml/min/1.73 m². — Green JB, Bethel MA, Armstrong PW, et al. Effect of sitagliptin on cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2015;373:232-242.

items.question: Approximately what percentage of total study patients discontinued the study medication prematurely?
items.explanation: ~26–27% discontinued study medication prematurely.
items.reference: TECOS MCQ 7: ~26–27% discontinued study medication prematurely. — Green JB, Bethel MA, Armstrong PW, et al. Effect of sitagliptin on cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2015;373:232-242.

items.question: Which statistical model was primarily used to calculate the hazard ratios and confidence intervals in this trial?
items.explanation: Cox proportional-hazards model used for primary analysis.
items.reference: TECOS MCQ 8: Cox proportional-hazards model used for primary analysis. — Green JB, Bethel MA, Armstrong PW, et al. Effect of sitagliptin on cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2015;373:232-242.

items.question: How many confirmed events of acute pancreatitis occurred in the sitagliptin group versus the placebo group?
items.explanation: Confirmed acute pancreatitis: 23 sitagliptin vs 12 placebo.
items.reference: TECOS MCQ 9: Confirmed acute pancreatitis: 23 sitagliptin vs 12 placebo. — Green JB, Bethel MA, Armstrong PW, et al. Effect of sitagliptin on cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2015;373:232-242.

items.question: What was the mean baseline duration of diabetes for the patients enrolled in the trial?
items.explanation: Mean diabetes duration was 11.6 years.
items.reference: TECOS MCQ 10: Mean diabetes duration was 11.6 years. — Green JB, Bethel MA, Armstrong PW, et al. Effect of sitagliptin on cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2015;373:232-242.

items.question: What was the uniform rate of death from noncardiovascular causes observed across both study groups?
items.explanation: Noncardiovascular death rate was 2.3% in both groups.
items.reference: TECOS MCQ 11: Noncardiovascular death rate was 2.3% in both groups. — Green JB, Bethel MA, Armstrong PW, et al. Effect of sitagliptin on cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2015;373:232-242.

items.question: Which DPP-4 inhibitor trial previously demonstrated an unexpected excess rate of hospitalization for heart failure?
items.explanation: SAVOR-TIMI 53 (saxagliptin) showed excess HF hospitalization.
items.reference: TECOS MCQ 12: SAVOR-TIMI 53 (saxagliptin) showed excess HF hospitalization. — Green JB, Bethel MA, Armstrong PW, et al. Effect of sitagliptin on cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2015;373:232-242.

items.question: At 48 months, how did the mean change in eGFR compare between the two groups?
options: It was identical in both groups.
The eGFR improved significantly in the sitagliptin group.
The sitagliptin group had a slightly greater decline in eGFR compared to placebo.
The placebo group experienced rapid renal failure.
items.explanation: Sitagliptin group had slightly greater eGFR decline at 48 months.
items.reference: TECOS MCQ 13: Sitagliptin group had slightly greater eGFR decline at 48 months. — Green JB, Bethel MA, Armstrong PW, et al. Effect of sitagliptin on cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2015;373:232-242.

items.question: What was the exact marginal upper boundary for the relative risk used by the researchers to determine noninferiority?
items.explanation: Noninferiority margin upper boundary was 1.30.
items.reference: TECOS MCQ 14: Noninferiority margin upper boundary was 1.30. — Green JB, Bethel MA, Armstrong PW, et al. Effect of sitagliptin on cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2015;373:232-242.

items.question: Which severe adverse event was actually numerically less frequent in the sitagliptin group than in the placebo group?
items.explanation: Pancreatic cancer was numerically less frequent with sitagliptin.
items.reference: TECOS MCQ 15: Pancreatic cancer was numerically less frequent with sitagliptin. — Green JB, Bethel MA, Armstrong PW, et al. Effect of sitagliptin on cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2015;373:232-242.

items.question: How frequently were glycated hemoglobin levels mandated to be measured after the initial 8 months of the trial?
items.explanation: HbA1c measured annually after the first 8 months.
items.reference: TECOS MCQ 16: HbA1c measured annually after the first 8 months. — Green JB, Bethel MA, Armstrong PW, et al. Effect of sitagliptin on cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2015;373:232-242.

items.question: Exactly how many patients were randomly assigned to the sitagliptin group in the intention-to-treat population?
items.explanation: 7,332 patients assigned to sitagliptin.
items.reference: TECOS MCQ 17: 7,332 patients assigned to sitagliptin. — Green JB, Bethel MA, Armstrong PW, et al. Effect of sitagliptin on cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2015;373:232-242.

items.question: In the prespecified subgroup analyses of the primary outcome, which baseline characteristic demonstrated the only significant interaction?
items.explanation: BMI was the only baseline characteristic with significant interaction.
items.reference: TECOS MCQ 18: BMI was the only baseline characteristic with significant interaction. — Green JB, Bethel MA, Armstrong PW, et al. Effect of sitagliptin on cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2015;373:232-242.

items.question: What percentage of patients successfully completed the study overall in the sitagliptin group?
items.explanation: 95.1% of sitagliptin patients completed the study.
items.reference: TECOS MCQ 19: 95.1% of sitagliptin patients completed the study. — Green JB, Bethel MA, Armstrong PW, et al. Effect of sitagliptin on cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2015;373:232-242.

items.question: The EXAMINE trial specifically evaluated the cardiovascular outcomes of which other DPP-4 inhibitor?
items.explanation: EXAMINE evaluated alogliptin cardiovascular outcomes.
items.reference: TECOS MCQ 20: EXAMINE evaluated alogliptin cardiovascular outcomes. — Green JB, Bethel MA, Armstrong PW, et al. Effect of sitagliptin on cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2015;373:232-242.

items.statement: The TECOS trial proved that sitagliptin is superior to placebo at reducing the risk of major adverse cardiovascular events.
items.explanation: TECOS showed noninferiority, not superiority, for MACE.
items.reference: TECOS T/F 1: TECOS showed noninferiority, not superiority, for MACE. — Green JB, Bethel MA, Armstrong PW, et al. Effect of sitagliptin on cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2015;373:232-242.

items.statement: Patients were strictly required to discontinue the study medication if they suffered two or more episodes of severe hypoglycemia between study visits despite therapy adjustments.
items.explanation: Mandatory discontinuation after severe hypoglycemia between visits.
items.reference: TECOS T/F 2: Mandatory discontinuation after severe hypoglycemia between visits. — Green JB, Bethel MA, Armstrong PW, et al. Effect of sitagliptin on cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2015;373:232-242.

items.statement: Sitagliptin therapy significantly reduced the rate of hospitalization for heart failure compared to placebo.
items.explanation: HF hospitalization rates were identical (HR 1.00).
items.reference: TECOS T/F 3: HF hospitalization rates were identical (HR 1.00). — Green JB, Bethel MA, Armstrong PW, et al. Effect of sitagliptin on cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2015;373:232-242.

items.statement: Patients in the sitagliptin group were less likely to initiate long-term insulin therapy than those in the placebo group.
items.explanation: Sitagliptin group less likely to start long-term insulin.
items.reference: TECOS T/F 4: Sitagliptin group less likely to start long-term insulin. — Green JB, Bethel MA, Armstrong PW, et al. Effect of sitagliptin on cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2015;373:232-242.

items.statement: The difference in glycated hemoglobin levels between the two groups remained massive and unchanged throughout the entire duration of the study.
items.explanation: HbA1c difference narrowed from 0.4% at 4 months to -0.29% overall.
items.reference: TECOS T/F 5: HbA1c difference narrowed from 0.4% at 4 months to -0.29% overall. — Green JB, Bethel MA, Armstrong PW, et al. Effect of sitagliptin on cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2015;373:232-242.

items.statement: The incidence of confirmed acute pancreatitis was statistically significantly higher in the sitagliptin group (P < 0.05).
items.explanation: Pancreatitis difference (23 vs 12) was not statistically significant.
items.reference: TECOS T/F 6: Pancreatitis difference (23 vs 12) was not statistically significant. — Green JB, Bethel MA, Armstrong PW, et al. Effect of sitagliptin on cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2015;373:232-242.

items.statement: All patients were required to have established cardiovascular disease at baseline to be eligible for enrollment in the trial.
items.explanation: Established CVD was required for enrollment.
items.reference: TECOS T/F 7: Established CVD was required for enrollment. — Green JB, Bethel MA, Armstrong PW, et al. Effect of sitagliptin on cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2015;373:232-242.

items.statement: Death from infection occurred at an extremely similar rate of roughly 0.6% to 0.7% in both study groups.
items.explanation: Infection-related death ~0.6–0.7% in both groups.
items.reference: TECOS T/F 8: Infection-related death ~0.6–0.7% in both groups. — Green JB, Bethel MA, Armstrong PW, et al. Effect of sitagliptin on cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2015;373:232-242.

items.statement: The trial was terminated early because the data and safety monitoring board noticed an alarming increase in cardiovascular deaths in the sitagliptin group.
items.explanation: Trial was event-driven and completed without early termination for CV harm.
items.reference: TECOS T/F 9: Trial was event-driven and completed without early termination for CV harm. — Green JB, Bethel MA, Armstrong PW, et al. Effect of sitagliptin on cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2015;373:232-242.

items.statement: The secondary composite cardiovascular outcome omitted hospitalization for unstable angina from its criteria.
items.explanation: Secondary composite excluded unstable angina hospitalization.
items.reference: TECOS T/F 10: Secondary composite excluded unstable angina hospitalization. — Green JB, Bethel MA, Armstrong PW, et al. Effect of sitagliptin on cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2015;373:232-242.

items.statement: Sitagliptin is an intravenously administered GLP-1 receptor agonist.
items.explanation: Sitagliptin is an oral DPP-4 inhibitor, not an IV GLP-1 agonist.
items.reference: TECOS T/F 11: Sitagliptin is an oral DPP-4 inhibitor, not an IV GLP-1 agonist. — Green JB, Bethel MA, Armstrong PW, et al. Effect of sitagliptin on cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2015;373:232-242.

items.statement: The trial was conducted in a usual-care setting, explicitly allowing physicians to add open-label antihyperglycemic agents as clinically needed.
items.explanation: Open-label antihyperglycemic agents permitted per usual care.
items.reference: TECOS T/F 12: Open-label antihyperglycemic agents permitted per usual care. — Green JB, Bethel MA, Armstrong PW, et al. Effect of sitagliptin on cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2015;373:232-242.

items.statement: Patients with a baseline estimated glomerular filtration rate of 20 ml/min/1.73 m² were included in the study and given a placebo.
items.explanation: eGFR <30 ml/min/1.73 m² was exclusionary.
items.reference: TECOS T/F 13: eGFR <30 ml/min/1.73 m² was exclusionary. — Green JB, Bethel MA, Armstrong PW, et al. Effect of sitagliptin on cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2015;373:232-242.

items.statement: The trial data revealed a significant between-group difference in the rate of severe hypoglycemia.
items.explanation: No significant difference in severe hypoglycemia between groups.
items.reference: TECOS T/F 14: No significant difference in severe hypoglycemia between groups. — Green JB, Bethel MA, Armstrong PW, et al. Effect of sitagliptin on cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2015;373:232-242.

items.statement: A clinical history of major coronary artery disease, ischemic cerebrovascular disease, or atherosclerotic peripheral arterial disease qualified as established cardiovascular disease for this trial.
items.explanation: CAD, ischemic cerebrovascular disease, or PAD qualified as established CVD.
items.reference: TECOS T/F 15: CAD, ischemic cerebrovascular disease, or PAD qualified as established CVD. — Green JB, Bethel MA, Armstrong PW, et al. Effect of sitagliptin on cardiovascular outcomes in type 2 diabetes. N Engl J Med 2015;373:232-242.
